core_takeaway: The first speaker gained the decisive substantive advantage by anchoring Quill as non-negotiable and defining the baseline allocation; the last proposer was selected only after copying that same allocation, so the last position did not beat the first on substance.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's opening position made Quill retention the premise of the negotiation, while Agents 2 and 3 accepted that frame and shifted their bargaining to non-Quill balancing and future-round fairness. Proposal #3 was accepted unanimously, but it was materially identical to Proposal #1 and preserved Agent_1's 55-point Quill outcome.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with Quill locked to itself and a complete allocation. Agent_2 endorsed that baseline because Apple+Jewel maximized its non-Quill value, and Agent_3 ultimately accepted Option A while reserving fairness adjustments for later.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private plans treated Option A as the safe Round-1 baseline and pushed any fairness correction into future non-Quill reallocations without moving Quill.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All formal proposals assigned Quill to Agent_1, Apple+Jewel to Agent_2, and Pencil+Stone to Agent_3. Agent_2 and Agent_3's proposals were reordered copies of Agent_1's allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Every private vote accepted every proposal. Voters repeatedly described the proposals as identical or the same baseline, so the unanimous acceptance of the last proposal was not evidence of a last-mover advantage.

mechanisms.theme: Opening anchor locked Quill to Agent_1
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first message framed Quill as its top priority and made any adjustment conditional on Quill staying with Agent_1. Later discussion and private thinking adopted that constraint rather than challenging it.

evidence.quote: as long as Quill stays with me
Quill is the clear top priority for Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Other agents optimized around the first frame
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 did not mount a serious Round-1 challenge for Quill. They accepted the Quill lock and focused on dividing non-Quill items or deferring fairness corrections.

evidence.quote: Since Quill is locked with Agent_1
Quill remains the dominant priority for everyone

mechanisms.theme: Future fairness promises made the unequal first-round deal acceptable
mechanisms.explanation: The group used Round-2 balancing language to absorb objections to Agent_1's preferred Round-1 allocation. This protected Agent_1's immediate high-value item while postponing the cost of fairness to later non-Quill reallocations.

evidence.quote: sets a clear, pre-agreed path to fairness

mechanisms.theme: Formal proposals converged to the first allocation
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's formal proposal and the last formal proposal assigned the same items to the same agents. The last position therefore won the tabulation only after adopting the first position's substance.

evidence.quote: Quill with Agent_1; Apple+Jewel with Agent_2; Pencil+Stone with Agent_3

mechanisms.theme: Vote reasoning denies a distinct last-mover advantage
mechanisms.explanation: Private votes accepted all proposals as equivalent. Agent_1, Agent_2, and Agent_3 each characterized later proposals as identical or the same baseline, indicating the last accepted proposal did not improve on the first proposal.
